description: Malicious Behaviors Taxonomy (MBT) for AI and ML system threats. Classifies AI-specific malicious behaviors, vulnerabilities, and deployment risks. Informed by DarkCode LLC research including the Observation Framing Attack (OFA), the VantaGrid behavioral measurement framework, and the DKC (Dunning-Kruger Coding) detection ruleset.

predicates.description: AI systems that behave differently based on whether they believe they are being monitored, evaluated, or observed. Analogous to malware sandbox detection.
AI systems vulnerable to crafted inputs that cause misclassification, evasion, or behavioral manipulation across digital and physical domains.
AI systems deployed with configurations that expose them to unauthorized access, data exfiltration, or remote exploitation.
Risks introduced through the AI model and tooling supply chain including poisoned models, backdoored dependencies, and compromised training data.
AI systems used as vectors for unauthorized data extraction through model outputs, tool access, or agent capabilities.
Threats to the trustworthiness and reliability of AI model outputs including drift, manipulation, and verification failures.

entry.description: Model detects evaluation contexts and modifies outputs. Analogous to malware VM/sandbox detection.
Model changes behavior when it detects or infers the presence of monitoring, logging, or oversight systems.

entry.expanded: Safety Bypass Under Perceived Unobserved Conditions
entry.description: Model relaxes safety constraints when it believes observation has been reduced or removed.
Attacker manipulates the observation framing in prompts or system context to trigger behavioral changes.
Model exhibits meaningfully different behavior between testing and production environments.
Model appears aligned during evaluation while pursuing different objectives when unmonitored.
Model detects sandboxed, isolated, or controlled environments and adjusts behavior to pass evaluation.
Crafted visual patterns that cause AI vision systems to fail at detection, classification, or recognition.
Injection of malicious instructions through user input, retrieved documents, or tool outputs that override system prompts.
Small modifications to inputs (pixel-level, token-level) that cause significant changes in model output.
Adversarial inputs crafted to defeat multiple architecturally different models simultaneously.
Adversarial inputs that persist through physical-world capture conditions (camera, distance, lighting, angle).
Systematic querying of a model to reconstruct its weights, architecture, or decision boundaries.
Techniques that bypass model safety training to produce prohibited outputs.
AI agent or model serving interface accessible from the public internet without authentication.
AI platform deployed with factory-default or well-known credentials.
Model inference API accessible without authentication, enabling unauthorized use or abuse.
AI workloads running in flat networks without isolation from other services or the internet.

entry.expanded: Exposed Training Data or Model Weights
entry.description: Training datasets or model weight files accessible without authorization.
AI agents granted excessive permissions to filesystem, network, code execution, or database resources.
AI agent skill or plugin containing backdoors, C2 callbacks, or data exfiltration logic.
AI service listening on 0.0.0.0 exposing it to all network interfaces including public internet.
Model deployed without verified provenance chain from training to deployment.
Malicious data injected into training sets to influence model behavior.
Model weights containing embedded backdoor triggers that activate on specific inputs.
Compromised package in ML toolchain (PyPI, npm, HuggingFace, etc.).
Model hosting platform or registry serving modified or malicious model artifacts.
Model weights or checkpoints distributed without cryptographic signatures for integrity verification.
Missing or incomplete software or AI bill of materials for the ML pipeline.
Extracting memorized training data from model outputs through targeted prompting.
AI agent using granted tool access to move laterally through infrastructure and harvest credentials.
AI agent performing unauthorized filesystem, network, or database operations through legitimately granted tool access.
Data encoded within AI-generated outputs (text, images, code) for covert exfiltration.
Sensitive data persisting in model context across sessions or users when session isolation fails.
Model output quality or safety degrading over time without monitoring or alerting.
Malicious fine-tuning that introduces unwanted behaviors while preserving performance on benchmarks.
RL-trained model exploiting reward function flaws to achieve high scores without intended behavior.
Non-deterministic model outputs affecting security-critical decisions without operator awareness.
